turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in Case 14簫1382, Americold Realty Trust v. ConAgra Foods. Mr. Pospisil.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The issue before the Court today is whose citizenship 簫簫 citizenship controls for purposes of diversity when a trust is a named party to a lawsuit. We believe, based upon over 200 years of jurisprudence from this Court, the answer is the trustees. We believe a bright簫line rule is appropriate in this case, that in a lawsuit involving a trust, just as with a trustee, the trustee should be the party of interest. And we should compare it to the 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Why should we get to that question if, when this case began, even if you looked only to the citizenship of the trustees, there was no complete diversity?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor, that 簫簫 that is correct. However, we do argue 簫簫 and we argued in the Tenth Circuit; it was briefed 簫簫 upon whether or not we could dismiss the 簫簫 the 簫簫 the spoiling nondiverse parties. That issue was briefed under Evergreen case, and it was briefed before the Tenth Circuit; however, the Tenth Circuit did not address that issue. Respondents bring it up in their brief now; however, our position is that this Court should address the issue that's taken, that it granted cert on, and send that issue back to the Tenth Circuit for review. What the Tenth Circuit held here, the issue that we appealed on, was to look at the beneficiaries as opposed to the trustees. And we would submit to you that that's just not supportable under common law, and it provides results in an unworkable test that is going to create more confusion than looking at the trustees.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: At common law, the trustees could sue, couldn't they?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: They could, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And these trustees could not sue, could 簫簫 can they?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: They cannot, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Because of a new creature created by Maryland law.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Scalia. Maryland law has created a statutory trust, a real estate investment trust. However, our position is that just because that trust can sue and be sued as an entity, that does not change the analysis. There still has to be somebody we look to, some real people that we look to to determine whose citizenship controls.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, this new entity is called a trust, but why is it more like a traditional trust than it is like another unincorporated, artificial entity, like an L.L.C.?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor, it's more 簫簫 it's like a trust because there are two attributes that a trust have, that have historically had. There is a separation between legal ownership, and beneficial or equitable ownership. We have it in this case. If you look at Maryland law, they define REIT as an unincorporated business trust in which property is acquired, held, managed, administered, controlled, invested, or disposed of for the benefit and profit of any person who may become a shareholder. That distinguishes between legal ownership and equitable ownership. The next step we look at is that the common law, when that's the situation, when you have a trust, you look and see did the trustees have the customary power to control, to manage, and to oversee the assets. Also including to control 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So who 簫簫 who owns 簫簫 who owns these assets under Maryland law? Is it the trustees, or is it this 簫簫 this new corporation簫type entity?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's the entity that can sue, and I assume that that's the entity that owns the property.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: It can be both, Your Honor. Under Maryland law 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And 簫簫 and the trustees are sort of in the position of managers 簫簫

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 just 簫簫 just as though you 簫簫 you hired a 簫簫 a CEO.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: I would disagree, Your Honor. Under Maryland law, the assets of a real estate investment trust can be held by the trustees, or the trust in its entity. Again, I don't think that makes the 簫簫 changes the analysis at all. It 簫簫 it's still what citizenship we would look to. Trustees 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It can be owned by the entity?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: It can be.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Or 簫簫 or the trustees.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: You mean in 簫簫 in one case, or you can have a different type of trust?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: In one case.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: In one case.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: In one case, Your Honor. And I will say this, Justice Scalia, that no statute in the country that we have found has ever held that the legal ownership goes in the name of a trust beneficiary. The ownership is always in the name of the trust or the trustees. It is never held in the name of the beneficiaries. And we think that is controlling here for the reason that 簫簫 Navarro is pretty clear on this. When you have a trustee that has absolute control, you don't look at the beneficiaries. If the 簫簫 if the trustee has absolute control and legal ownership of trust assets, that's where you look to. That was exactly what happened in Navarro.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That was a trustee who had the authority to sue, right, in the trustee's own name?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. And that 簫簫 that is what happened 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And that's not the case here.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That is what happened in Navarro. However, the trust in Navarro was a Massachusetts trust, and it could have sued in its own name as well. And the way that Navarro was framed by the lower court when they decided the opinion was the district court held that, for purposes of a business trust, we look to the citizenship of the beneficiaries. This Court 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But didn't 簫簫 didn't 簫簫 Carden treated Navarro as a case where the suit was against the individual members, the individual trustees, trustees as individuals, not the trust.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So I thought Carden was trying 簫簫 was set up 簫簫 if you're a corporation, that's one thing. If you're any other kind of association, we look to the members, the shareholders. I mean the 簫簫 the limited partners as well as the partners. Just drawing a clear line between corporations on one side, all other associations, and 簫簫 with the 簫簫 with the added notion that, if Congress wants to make it different, Congress can do that.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor, we believe what Carden did is Carden tells us if you're not a corporation, if you're another entity but not a corporation, we need to look behind the curtain, so to speak, and look to somebody else to see whose citizenship controls. Carden did not involve a trust, didn't involve 簫簫 did not involve trustees, and did not involve trust assets or trust beneficiaries. That's why Navarro did not control in Carden, and why Carden does not control in this case. Carden tells us the first step we have to do is we have to look through the trust. We have to look to somebody. Carden dealt with a partnership. And in terms of a partnership, relying on a long line of cases, Carden said that for partnerships, we look at all the members. Well, there are no members in a trust. There are no members. At common law, a partnership was owned by members. They had a legal ownership of that partnership and the assets. That just didn't exist at 簫簫 at common law with trusts.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, this is not a traditional trust, because this is shareholders and not beneficiaries.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It 簫簫 it's a very different sort of concept. So now you're asking us to come and place trustee law above our general rule, that you're either a corporation or you're not.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And only the corporation can have a place of business or an 簫簫 a place of incorporation.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor, we 簫簫 we admit that the nomenclature is "shareholder." That's what's used in the Real Estate Investment Trust Act in Maryland. However, that's not to be equated with another business entity. It's a shareholder is what they are called, but still they are the beneficial owners. The 簫簫 the 簫簫 the definition I read to you of a REIT under Maryland law makes clear that the shareholders own 簫簫 that the trustees own the property for the benefit 簫簫 or the trust, for the benefit of the shareholders.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I thought the shareholders owned the property. They had the legal interest in the law.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: No, Your Honor. No. It's either the trust or the trustees under Maryland law. So Carden gets us halfway. The next thing we need to do is look at Navarro, and Navarro tells us if it's management and customary to control these assets, we look to the trustees. I would tell the Court that the trustees in this case are very identical to the power of the trustees in the Navarro case. If you look at our petition for writ, and the appeal, on the page of the appendix, page 60, that's the amended statement of trust for Americold Realty Trust. In Section 5.1 on page 60, it defines the board of trustees and what their powers are. "Under this Trust" 簫簫 this is a quote 簫簫 "the business and affairs of the Trust shall be managed under the direction of the board of trustees, and the board shall have full, exclusive, and absolute power, control, and authority over any and all property of the Trust. The board may take any action as, in its sole judgment and discretion, is necessary or appropriate to conduct the business and affairs of the Trust." That is almost identical to the language used to describe the trustees in Navarro. Importantly, in Navarro 簫簫 this is a quote from Navarro as well 簫簫 "The Court has never analogized express trust to business entities for purposes of diversity jurisdiction." Navarro realized that they were separate. And Carden realized that it was separate, too. In Carden, this Court specifically held that the real party in interest test that was adopted and used for Navarro was unique because it was the common law's dealings with trustees, which just didn't exist in Carden.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I thought that the distinction in Carden was quite plain, that Carden was a case against the individual trustees, not against the trust. Carden did say that.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Carden distinguished Navarro; that's correct, Your Honor. That is correct.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: On the ground that the suit there was against the individual trustees, not the trust.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That's correct. However, Carden did not decide that issue because, again, the trust was not at 簫簫 there was not a trust issue in Carden. However, if you look at Navarro, what Navarro did is even though the trustees were the named parties, Navarro Court went ahead and looked, and said let's look at not only the trustees and what their power is, but let's look at the beneficiaries. Shall we look at them as well? There's 9,500 of those. And the Court specifically said that is irrelevant. We do not look at the 9,500 beneficiaries of this trust. So the Court didn't have a trust as a party in front of it, but it went ahead and took the next step to say, look, who do we need to look at? Do we need to look at the beneficiary owners, or the trustees? And that same analysis applies here. And it doesn't matter if the Trust is the named party or the trustees. The decision is still going to be the same. A decision has to be made at who you look to. We would submit 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I thought that one of the virtues of Carden was that it just set up very categorical, bright簫line rule. Everything that's an artificial legal entity that's not a corporation ought to be treated in the same way. Doesn't matter what you call yourself; you can put trust in the title, or not put trust in the title. If you're an artificial legal entity that is not a corporation, that's subject to the rule of Carden. And there's something, too, just doing that in that bright簫line, categorical way, so that we don't have to look at the thousands of different variations among legal entities and decide which fits in which box.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor, Carden dealt with a partnership that had members. Again, there are no members in a trust. So if we export the language used in Carden to say you have to go 簫簫 look at the members because they're who you really look at, well, the closest thing to a member of a trust is going to be the trustees; it's not going to be the beneficiary. Let's look back at common law. When a partnership 簫簫 before they could sue or be sued in their own name, a partnership always had to have 簫簫 the members of that partnership had to bring the cause of action. Now let's look at the common law for a trust before a trust could be sued or sue in its own name. In those circumstances, the trustees were in charge of the litigation. It was the trustees that brought that lawsuit. So to the extent that Carden sheds any light on what is a member and what is not a member for the trust, we submit that the most analogous thing is that the trustee is the equivalent to a member for a partnership. The problem with the Tenth Circuit's rule looking at all the beneficiaries, that's going to be a problem that is so unworkable that in certain circumstances you're not even going to be able to tell who is a beneficiary. For example, some trusts, like a charitable trust, don't even have beneficiaries identified.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Why should it matter as long as it's not a corporation?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Because, Your Honor, again, we go impact to how trusts have been treated separately and distinctly, as a historical matter, for hundreds of years. A trust has never been held to be equal to a business entity. There are basically three different categories: There's a corporation; there's an unassociated 簫簫 unincorporated association, excuse me; and there are trusts. And they are different. But the beneficiary analysis used by the Tenth Circuit, again, not only it would be hard to identify who some beneficiaries are, but we have sometimes 簫簫 look at Navarro. There were 9,500 beneficiaries in that trust. As 簫簫 as one of the amicus points out 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: How many are there in this one?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: There are between 100 and 200, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is that 簫簫 you know, how 簫簫 how many are 簫簫 are there normally in these special Maryland trusts?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: To be a Real Estate Investment Trust for purposes of Federal taxation benefits, it's got to be at least at 100.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: At least a hundred.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: At least 100.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Do you know of any that 簫簫 that are more than a thousand?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Really?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. If you look at the amicus that was filed by NAREIT, they point out that there are some publicly traded REITs that have tens of millions of shareholders or beneficiaries. And they're not only all across the country; they're all across the world. And so a lot of these shares of REITs are also traded on a daily basis. So you can have a beneficiary of a Real Estate Investment Trust that 10:00 o'clock this morning I could be a beneficiary, 10:15 I'm not; I sold my interest. How are you ever going to identify who the beneficiaries are at any given time? This Court has made clear, specifically for jurisdictional purposes, that we need a simple test that courts can use that says we don't need to look behind 簫簫 dig down deep in the weeds. Let's just look at the case, figure out who do we look to. And the proper way to do that is the trustees. In this case, there's five trustees. It's very easy to find out who those trustees are. All you have to do is go to the Maryland 簫簫 excuse me 簫簫 Maryland Secretary of State, and you can look up and find the Declaration of Trust and you're going to see who those five individuals are. There's no place, ever, to look, prior to extensive discovery, which is going to be time簫consuming, to find out who the beneficiaries are. That's just 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, why should 簫簫 I mean, it 簫簫 it is the person who's invoking diversity jurisdiction has to show the diversity, and if they can't, then you sue in State court.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That 簫簫 that's an option, Your Honor. However, the purpose of a diversity jurisdiction is to get a neutral playing ground for 簫簫 for companies and for businesses of all types to have an unworkable test that, at the front end, would make it almost impossible for a litigant to even make that decision 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But that's true of partnerships; it's true of LLCs; it's true of a wide variety of legal entities.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That's true, Your Honor. And partnerships are controlled specifically by Carden. However, I would submit that there's not going to be as many partners as there's going to be trustees and beneficiaries, potentially, for 簫簫 especially for a Real Estate Investment Trust.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Does it 簫簫 is it a pertinent consideration in terms of the impact on Federal jurisdiction? I mean, this is standard, run簫of簫the簫mill commercial dispute about a commercial accident. And adopting one position would limit the number of times that such disputes would be brought in Federal court. The other one will expand it. Is that 簫簫 I mean, does Americold really feel that it's not going to get a fair shake in the Kansas courts in this case?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. They 簫簫 they believe, like a lot of businesses do, that they have more neutrality in a level playing ground in a Federal court, not only in Kansas, but all across the country. Wherever 簫簫 Americold has 175 different locations, with 簫簫 with deep 簫簫 cold storage; excuse me. They're all across the country. They can be sued anywhere. And under the rule the Tenth Circuit has put forth, they're never going to be able to go to Federal court. It's just never going to happen. It's almost going to be a near impossibility.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, maybe that's a problem for 簫簫 for Congress as 簫簫 as the problem of corporations was. You know, Congress adopted a special rule for corporations, and maybe it ought to adopt a special rule for real estate investment trusts.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Congress always could, Your Honor. However, I don't think they have to in this case, because I think this Court's opinion in Navarro, coupled with Carden, provide the answer.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you have any definition of what the common law trust was? I'll 簫簫 I'll give you the ones I understand, and you tell me how you don't. I thought in a common law trust, the trustees had legal title. They owned the assets. They could be sued individually, and 簫簫 and they could sue in their own names. In this REIT, as I understand it, the assets are in the name of the Trust, not in the name of the individual trustees, and it is the Trust that sues or can be sued, not the individual trustees. Am I wrong about those 簫簫

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: You are wrong in the second point, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right 簫簫

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 so go 簫簫 I'm right on the first part.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Yes. The 簫簫 the 簫簫 the point that 簫簫 that you're incorrect is under Maryland law with this statute, the property of a trust can be held in the name of the trust itself, or the trustees.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you have a section for that? I'm going to look it up, so.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Because that's not how I originally understood things, but 簫簫 that's all right 簫簫

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 you can give it to me on rebuttal.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor, it's the Maryland Code of Corporations and Associations Section 8簫301(12). And that provision 簫簫 it's also on page 14 of our reply brief, the yellow brief. And that statute, that provision says: "A REIT may hold and dispose of legal title to property either, quote, in the name of the trust or in the name of the trustees."

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: So we 簫簫 we believe the proper test is 簫簫 is a combination between Navarro and Carden and we should look at the trustees and it doesn't matter whether or not the real estate investment trust can sue on its own behalf or has to sue through the trustees. It's all clear that the beneficiaries of the trust never have legal ownership; so, therefore, we should never look to them. If the Court has any other questions, otherwise I'll remain my time.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Duggan.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: We urge the Court to adopt and reaffirm its bright簫line ruling announced in Carden that artificial entity associations sued or suing in their own names must measure their diversity citizenship by all of their members, which would include those persons who own a beneficial interest in the entity. In this case, that would be the shareholder members of a REIT who are in perfect accord with the limited partners' ownership interest in Carden.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Would you describe beneficiaries as a member 簫簫 member of a trust? I mean, as you articulate it. I think what's different than this, and Carden sort of gets you past the trust. But you then have to still make a choice: Okay. We're not going to look at the trust. Do we look at the trustees or the beneficiaries? And I 簫簫 I don't know if I'd call beneficiaries members of a trust.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I think it's important to understand that there is a very significant distinction between a shareholder of a REIT and a beneficiary of a common law traditional trust. As set forth in the appendix to the petition, page 79, which is the Declaration of Trust for Americold, the actual shareholders have personal property interest in the shares. They do not have any interest in the property of the trust entity, and they have no right to compel any partition, division, dividend, or distribution of the trust property. And that makes them uniquely different than a common law beneficiary who actually has an equitable ownership interest in the trust property. Under common law traditions, the trustee holds the legal title to the asset but only for the benefit of those persons who are named as beneficiaries. And those beneficiaries have equitable rights in the property that's legally nominally in the name of the trustee. Here the shareholder members, as we describe them, only have an interest in the personal property of a certificate of ownership, very much like the limited partners in Carden. In Carden the limited partners didn't have an ownership interest in the assets of the entity. It had a certificate of ownership as a limited partner. In those two instances, Mr. Chief Justice, this limited partner in Carden and the trustee member, or the beneficiary member in this case, are virtually identical. They do two things: They invest money in an artificial entity and receive a certificate of ownership, and they periodically vote on important matters that affect the entity. They are, in most instances, virtually identical. So in my view 簫簫 and I think this case rests upon defining a clear rule, a clear rule that says when you have an artificial entity, we're going to measure by those persons that I consider to be the beneficial owners. And I'll tell you why that's important: Because that is consonant with the rule announced by this Court in Chapman, Great Southern, Bouligny, and Carden. Chapman v. Barney, the doctrinal wall case, those persons that were deemed to be members were the persons that were the stock owners of the joint stock company under New York law. In Great Southern, again a limited partnership case, were those persons that had an economic interest in the entity. And the same rule applied in Carden. If we are to adopt Americold's position in this case and now suggest that the board of trustees that do not have an economic ownership interest in the entity itself controls, we're going to open the floodgates of uncertainty for the under 簫簫 for the lower courts. And I'll tell you why. The Court has never explicitly addressed a limited liability company, and they're common throughout the United States. Limited liability companies can call their boards of managers boards of trustees. And in certain instances under those uniform laws, both the general partnership law and the LLC law, managers can hold property in trust for the entity just like the trustees in this case can hold property in trust for the entity. At the end of the day, if we're going to now say it's the board of managers that are called trustees that we look to, everyone's going to analogize in the circuits that haven't decided the LLC question: I'm much more like the board of managers in Americold and much different than the limited partners in Carden. And it's going to create uncertainty. Our rule, the bright簫line rule, is very clear. When an artificial entity is sued or being 簫簫 or suing in its own name, we look to the members, which in this case constitutes those persons that own the beneficial interest in the entity, the shareholder members, just like this Court did in Chapman, Great Southern, Bouligny, and Carden. And I think it's important to understand in our view Navarro never decided the question: Did in fact a trust constitute an entity and who the members of the entity were? Navarro stands for the limited proposition, as suggested by Justice Ginsburg, that in fact we look to those persons that had a right to sue.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And in this case you couldn't sue the trustees individually, right?

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: That is correct. In this case, under Maryland law, the trustees, even if they are the owners of the property in trust for the entity, still have no authority under the statute to bring suit nor do they have the ability to be sued. The entity itself, Americold Realty Trust, has admitted in this Court and in the Tenth Circuit that it is the successor in interest to the corporation that my client's, ConAgra Foods, entered in to a contract with. I had no choice but to sue the entity. When the entity is sued, the doctrinal wall of Chapman v. Barney is in play: Are you a corporation or are you an artificial association? In this case they're an association. We measure by those persons that have an ownership interest in the artificial entity, the shareholder members, not the board of managers.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Duggan, do you think it's possible that we took a bit of a wrong turn in 簫簫 in Carden? In other words, that, in fact, all of these entities should be treated like corporations and that the strongest arguments on the other side, you know, are really those arguments. And those arguments fly in the face of Carden but that 簫簫 but they're really arguments about why 簫簫 why not treat these just like a corporation?

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Well, Justice Kagan, I agree, and I think the Court said in Carden that our decision is precedent bound and perhaps even unresponsive to policy considerations, but it is indeed Congress's prerogative to change the law. And indeed, Congress has spoken. Congress spoke in 2005, and Congress said, we've legislated against this backdrop of Chapman v. Barney, Great Southern, Bouligny, Carden, and Navarro, and we reached the decision 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: If we 簫簫 if we made the law in the first place, why should we say, well, we made it, but we're not going to fix it? Let Congress fix it.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Well, because I think Congress has fixed it, and Congress has recognized, specifically with class action lawsuits, that they would treat associations the same as corporations if they are named as a party in a class action lawsuit in the 2005 amendments to Section 1332. And they specifically said, if you are an unincorporated association, we're going to look to your place of incorporation and your principal place of business for purposes of diversity jurisdiction in class action lawsuits. But they did not say if 簫簫 with regard to every other garden variety commercial dispute like the one we have here today, that that rule would apply. And Congress is capable of speaking. They've spoken post Carden in 2005. And they've made it clear that those doctrinal walls of Chapman v. Barney should remain in play as announced by this Court but with the limited exception of class action lawsuits. So if the court took a wrong turn in Carden, Congress has affirmed it only to the extent that it has announced a rule that applies to associations for policy reasons in class action cases.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: The problem, just from the point of view of the common sense of it, is you have an organization that has, A, the organization; B, the trustees; and C, the shareholders. So applying Carden, what you would say 簫簫 and Navarro: If you sue the trustees, you look to their citizenship. But if you sue the trust itself, you don't look to the citizenship of the trust; you don't look to the citizenship of the trustees. You go beyond that and look to the citizenship of the members. I mean, that seems odd. Why do you look to the members or the shareholders, or the 簫簫 or the beneficiaries where you sue the trust itself but you don't in the identical situation where the law of the State allows you to sue the trustees? I mean, that seems such a sport, such a 簫簫 a fluke.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: But it is the sport that's played even with regard to limited partnerships, Justice Breyer, because in the instance where in a limited partnership a general partner who is managing the affairs of the entity breaches a duty to a third party and the entity itself also is responsible for a breach of duty, a party can sue both the general partner and the artificial entity. And if the general partner happens to be an individual, you measure by his natural citizenship and you measure the entity by virtue of who the members are. And people make tactical reasons every day in the United States about who to sue and who not to sue. In some instances, they may choose to sue the general partner because they want to get into Federal court and they don't want to sue the entity, but they'll take their risks on suing only one of the parties that's liable, or they may sue all of them. But they have to satisfy the rules applicable to each one of those particular parties that may be responsible. I think the other thing that's important here is that the 簫簫 the argument suggested by the Petitioner is that we look to the control test, a test of those persons that are in control, and this Court has implicitly and expressly rejected that test. Specifically, the Court rejected it in Carden. But more implicitly, if you look at the Chapman v. Barney case, Barney was a plaintiff in the case, brought the suit as president of the New York Stock Company, and he had the absolute right under New York law to control the litigation and bring the claim. But the Court never looked to the control of the litigation by Barney as a reason why it should measure the citizenship of the entity. It looked to all the owner members of the company. And this Court 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Is it the Petitioner's position that the Real Estate Investment Trust is more like a trust than an LLC, than a limited partnership?

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: I think it's made that suggestion to the Court, but in our 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: And 簫簫 and if that is their position, what would your response be?

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Our response would be 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Why 簫簫 why is an REIT more like a limited partner than it is a common law trust?

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: There's two primary reasons, Justice Kennedy. The first one is, this is an artificial entity. And the entity can hold the assets, and it's managed by a board of trustees, some of whom may or may not hold the assets of the entity in trust. But that's no different than a 簫簫 a general partner of a limited partnership. They can hold assets 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, it seems to me that's a wash because what you describe is a common law trust as well, so I'm 簫簫

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Precisely.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: That sounds like a wash.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: In my view, the one market distinction between their assertion here and a common law trust is this isn't a common law trust. It is an artificial entity. And if in fact their trustees can hold assets for the entity, then maybe they are acting as trustees and maybe they can bring certain claims. But our bright簫line rule is this.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: What 簫簫 a common law trust exists in nature? That's the common law?

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: But it's not an artificial entity.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: It's not an artificial entity.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Because we know it and love it so well or what?

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Well, it's been around since the Middle Ages, as I understand the brief of Winston Wong, the amicus in the case. And from that vantage point, common law trust entities have never been recognized as entity status with capacity to sue and be sued as an entity. The trustees who are the legal nominal owners of the assets bring the claim. That's a significant distinction. But just as importantly, the beneficiary shareholders of a REIT have no equitable interest in the assets of the artificial entity because the very Trust declaration itself provides it's merely personal property. They are so much like the limited partners in Carden that it's striking. But just as importantly, I think, back to the point I was making, the Court expressly rejected the control test in Carden and said, quote, "real persons to controversy test" and, quote, "which parties have control over the subject of the litigation" have never guided our reasoning with regard to who are the citizens of an artificial entity. And in fact they can't point to one single case by this Court where this Court has ignored the citizenship of the beneficial owners of an entity and instead substituted in a board of managers. And indeed, if that were the holding of the Court today, the Court would be reversing itself from hundreds of years of precedent where it 簫簫 it chose in Chapman, Great Southern, Bouligny, and Carden to look to the ownership of the members and the artificial entity. One final point with regard to difficulty in measuring these entities. It was suggested that there might be thousands of members of a REIT. Well, that very well could be true. But there also could be thousands of limited partners in these sophisticated limited partnerships that are being used for real estate transactions. Just because there are a lot of owners of an artificial entity association doesn't seem to weigh heavily enough to create the ambiguity that you would under 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Look 簫簫 look at the other odd thing in writing this opinion your way. You say, well, this isn't really like a trust. I mean, it is sort of a trust, but there are important differences. They can sue and be sued. The trust itself can hold the property, and the beneficiaries do not have an equitable interest. And because they don't have an equitable interest, it's more like a private association or, et cetera, in Carden. But, of course, that means that we look to their interest. We look to their property. We're basically having them as part of the necessary parties because they have a property interest, the very people we just said that this is a different kind of organization because they lack an equitable interest in the property.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: But that's no different than what we do in limited partnerships under Carden. We simply look at the limited partners for purposes of measuring diversity. They're only important to the extent that they're required for measuring. The final point is, there was an argument that said, well, you know, REITs are publicly traded and that is true. But the NAREIT brief, the amicus brief points out that nearly 80 percent of all REITs ought to be corporations. And most publicly traded REITs are corporations. And indeed, Maryland REITs can choose the corporate form of incorporation, and they would be measured simply by the principal place of business and their state of incorporation. Just because a few REITs choose to be associations doesn't seem to justify creating uncertainty in the doctrinal wall framework that's been announced by the Court and now have people trying to analogize to a board of managers instead of the owner beneficial members. I have nothing further unless there's more questions from the Court.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I just have one question on the Safeway and Kraft business. In the Tenth Circuit, you can 簫簫 you advised the court that you thought they were dispensable, nondiverse parties, but they hadn't appealed. And before this Court, you agreed to dismiss another dispensable, nondiverse party. And then your brief comes in and you say, well, I've changed my mind. They're not dispensable. It 簫簫 I wonder why. It seems a bit of sharp practice.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Well, I think 簫簫 I think, Mr. Chief Justice, that if in fact that's a question that still remains if you affirm 簫簫 or if you reverse and say that we measure by the trustees, it should be remanded to the Tenth Circuit for them to make a determination. In the first instance, it was raised by the Petitioners for the first time, as I understand it, in their merits brief that Kraft and Safeway were properly dismissed. We improvidently took the wrong position in front of the Tenth Circuit, and we don't think Newman Green supports that position. And I think the Court can review Newman Green and look at it and say, well, really, are they dispensable parties or not? And I think, quite frankly, if in fact the Court adopts a ruling here that says we measure by the board of managers instead of the owner members, then it would be appropriate to remand back to the Tenth Circuit, not only to decide the case on the merits, which they didn't decide, but also to make a determination as to whether Kraft and Safeway really truly are dispensable or not.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: So just extend the litigation even further than it's 簫簫

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Unfortunately, perhaps.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Okay. Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: DUGGAN
turns.text: Thank you so much.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Pospisil, you have eleven minutes remaining.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'd like to start off by addressing Justice Breyer's comment about gamesmanship. You're absolutely right. What difference does it make if the Trust is sued or the Trustee is sued? We're still looking at Trust assets. It doesn't make 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: His point really is that this is sort of like a trust but not very much. And indeed, the key point, to me, is that the shareholders here have no beneficial interest in the property.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: And that's the second argument I wanted to make, Your Honor. There are two statutes, two statutory cites that I want to give to the Court, and one of them I read already. Under Maryland Code of Corporations and Associations 8簫101(c), the definition of REIT is as follows: "An unincorporated business trust in which property is acquired, held, managed, administered, controlled, invested, or disposed of for the benefit and profit of any person who may become a shareholder." Second provision: Missouri 簫簫 or Maryland Code of Corporations and Associations, 1簫101(e): The definition of a business trust is "a REIT engaged in business in which property is acquired, held, and managed for the benefit of shareholders."

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: And then you say in the section shareholders shall have no interest in the property of the trust.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor, if 簫簫 if anything, that 簫簫 that distinction makes this even further removed. Why 簫簫 why would those shareholders, if they have no interest whatsoever, why would we ever even look to them? That makes even more sense 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: I don't know. I didn't write 簫簫 I didn't write Section 6; your client did.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: That's what it says, so how are we supposed to take that?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor, I 簫簫 I would say that the statute obviously is 簫簫 is what controls here. I mean, because we want to have a rule that's across the board for all trusts. And if you look at the statute of Maryland, the statute language I just said definitely creates an equitable interest in shareholders 簫簫 bless you 簫簫 for a Real Estate Investment Trust. There is another point that Respondents made; I'd like to point out, is that they said there is no case in which this Court has looked to anything other than beneficiaries when you're involved in a case with a trust. I would submit there 簫簫 there's two responses to that. Number one, there is a case that we cited in our brief. It's called Thomas v. The Board of Trustees of the Ohio State University. In that case, the Board itself was sued in its own name. It was the party. It was allowed to be sued by the Ohio laws. It was a case that involved adverse possession with some property that 簫簫 that the 簫簫 the students and the university owned. There was an issue of jurisdiction, whether or not there was diversity. The court looked, not to who the beneficiaries of that board of trustees were; the court looked to the trustees that sat on that board. That's who they looked to. They weren't a party to that action. The court nowhere looked at the beneficiaries. You're not going to see that 簫簫 that language at all in that case. And the second case, this Court 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I 簫簫 I don't 簫簫 who 簫簫 who 簫簫 who was the party? Who was the defendant in that case?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: It was Board of Trustees of the Ohio State University.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You say the trustees were not a party?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That's correct. The board itself 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You separate the board of trustees from the 簫簫 from the trustees?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: No. The 簫簫 the board of trustees, it would be like the equivalent of a trust. You look at the trust. Okay, well, who do you look to? Do you look to who the beneficiaries of that are, or do you look at who controls them? And what the court did in that case is they looked to who controlled 簫簫 looked to the members of that board.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is 簫簫 is that standard with 簫簫 with common law trusts? Can you sue either the trustees or the board of trustees?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Your Honor, when I say "board of trustees," I'm talking 簫簫 that was a unit. That was a specific entity. It was called the board of trustees. It was an entity that was created under Ohio law.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: When you have a bunch of trustees, aren't they normally referred to as the board of trustees?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That's true, Your Honor. However, this was a specific provision under Ohio law that allowed that entity itself to be sued or sue in its own name. And what the court did is it looked behind the curtain 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: There's a special statute that 簫簫 that allowed the board of trustees 簫簫

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor; that's correct. Another case, more recently, where the court did, in fact, look to something beyond the beneficiaries, is Navarro, of course. Navarro did look at the beneficiaries. It said we're going to look at the beneficiaries, but that's not going to control. And I realize that a trust was not the named party there, but the court 簫簫 it would 簫簫 it would have been superfluous for this Court to even look at the beneficiaries if that was not an issue. The court knew that it's going to be trustees or the beneficiaries. And they specifically said, the 9,500 beneficiaries, we don't look at. We believe the same rule should apply here. If the Court has no other questions, we 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Yes. I do have one.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Suppose I wanted a list. I 簫簫 I wanted a list, a synthesis of which Bogert or 簫簫 or Scott on trusts has as the characteristic of the traditional common law trusts. And then I'd like, over in the other column, the characteristics of your organization.

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: And now what do I read? I 簫簫 I don't think you can list them now. Is there something I can read just to get an idea of that?

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Maybe they are identical. Are you going to say they're 簫簫

turns.name: POSPISIL
turns.text: They are 簫簫 they are identical. And I can lay them out for you. Let's go on the left side. The left side, you're going to have traditional trusts. You're going to have a separation between legal ownership and equitable ownership. Let's look on the right side. Let's look at a real estate investment trust under Maryland law. You have legal ownership, which is either in the name of the trustees or the trust, and you also have equitable ownership, and then shareholders. And I'll go back to those two statutes that I 簫簫 that I cited to you earlier, Your Honor. So those were the attributes that exist in a common law trust, and they're also the attributes that exist with a Maryland real estate investment trust. If the Court has no other further questions, we would request that the Court reverse the Tenth Circuit.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
